input_candidates.prediction_value: At high temperature and high strain rate, the Fe-8%Al ferritic alloy undergoes discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX), producing randomly oriented grains that are advantageous for limiting roping defects.

input_candidates.truth_value: Figure 3: {1 1 0} pole figures of Fe–8% Al deformed by torsion show typical bcc torsion texture; strongest component D2: {-1-12}<111> is self-symmetric; maximum intensity of D2 observed at ε=15.
input_candidates.prediction_value: At lower temperature and strain rate, continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) occurs, associated with progressive transformation of subgrain boundaries into grain boundaries and development of the D2 {112}<111> texture component under negative shear.

input_candidates.truth_value: Figure 7: Strain rate–temperature map showing DDRX activation domain: 900°C/10 s^-1, 1000°C/10 s^-1, 1100°C/10 s^-1; DDRX not activated at lower strain rates (e.g., 900°C/1 s^-1, 1000°C/0.5 s^-1, 1100°C/0.1 s^-1).
input_candidates.prediction_value: Geometric dynamic recrystallization (GDRX) operates simultaneously with CDRX at large strains, where original high-angle boundaries become elongated and broken up ('pinch-off') when their thickness is sufficiently reduced.

input_candidates.truth_value: Roping defect reduction: To limit roping (inhomogeneous orientation distribution), it is advantageous to promote DDRX to achieve recrystallized grains with random orientations.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Strain rate–temperature map (Figure 7) delineating DDRX activation domain: at 900°C DDRX activates at 10 s⁻¹ (not at 1.0 s⁻¹); at 1000°C DDRX activates at 10 s⁻¹ (not at 0.5 s⁻¹); at 1100°C DDRX activates at 10 s⁻¹ (not at 0.1 s⁻¹).
